methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 2 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 19 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: QuantumScape
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $5B+

private.name: LanzaTech
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $800M+

data.narrative:
Enovix is a public-stage climate and energy company that has raised over $700 million. The valuation universe for this company is constrained: within Climate & Energy, there exists one public comparable (LanzaTech, ticker LNZA) and eight private peers including QuantumScape, TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, and Helion. This narrow peer set reflects the early-stage, capital-intensive nature of the sector.

Peer-median analysis was deemed inapplicable in this case. Although 19 same-sector peers exist within Climate & Energy broadly, filtering for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier companies yielded fewer than three datapoints—below the threshold for statistical reliability. A cross-sector median would introduce unacceptable methodological noise given Enovix's specialized capital requirements and commercialization timelines.

Without sufficient comparable company data, triangulation to a single valuation range cannot be performed with confidence. The analysis instead identifies key uncertainties that dominate any forward valuation: the depth and heterogeneity of the private comp set, commercial readiness relative to peers, and the degree to which public-market pricing of LNZA reflects sector-wide investor sentiment versus company-specific factors. Further valuation work would benefit from either narrowing the peer set to companies at identical commercialization stages or employing discounted cash flow or venture capital methods, both of which lie outside the scope of this peer-based analysis.
